* According to Watanabe et. al (2021), contemporary Kinki populations phenotypically differ the most from Jōmon people, especially Hokkaido Ainu. Other Japanese subpopulations were more intermediate although morphological similarities exist between Hokkaido Ainu and contemporary Tohoku populations.<ref name=":31">{{cite bioRxiv |last1=Watanabe |first1=Yusuke |last2=Ohashi |first2=Jun |title=Modern Japanese ancestry-derived variants revealed the formation process of the current Japanese regional gradations |date=2022 |biorxiv=10.1101/2020.12.07.414037 }}</ref>
* Several studies show quantifiable dimorphism in the cranium of Japanese people,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hoshioka |first1=Yumi |last2=Torimitsu |first2=Suguru |last3=Makino |first3=Yohsuke |last4=Yajima |first4=Daisuke |display-authors=3 |date=2025 |title=Sex estimation from skull measurements of a contemporary Japanese population using three-dimensional computed tomography images |journal=International Journal of Legal Medicine |volume=139 |issue=1 |pages=383–391 |doi=10.1007/s00414-024-03319-8 |pmid=39210043 |pmc=11732883 }}</ref><ref name=":2" /> similar to Thais, Indonesians, Filipinos and Malays.<ref name=":2" /> Japanese skulls also share some similarities with [[Hispanic and Latino Americans|Hispanics]] and [[Genetic and anthropology studies on Filipinos|Filipinos]],<ref name="NandarYuki">{{Cite thesis |last=Yukyi |first=Nandar |date=2 August 2017 |title=Craniometric Variation of Modern Asian and Hispanic Individuals Using Multivariate Analysis |url=https://digital.library.txst.edu/items/947989d4-87df-4d38-b643-51a1e3cdd80b |language=en}}</ref> implied by the frequency of [[Hispanic and Latino Americans|Hispanic]] skulls from southwestern United States being misclassified as Asian, particularly Japanese and [[Genetic and anthropology studies on Filipinos|Filipino]].<ref name="NandarYuki" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Torimitsu |first1=Suguru |last2=Nakazawa |first2=Akari |last3=Flavel |first3=Ambika |last4=Iwase |first4=Hirotaro |display-authors=3 |date=2024 |title=Estimation of population affinity using cranial measurements acquired in multidetector computed tomography images of Japanese and Malay individuals |journal=International Journal of Legal Medicine |volume=139 |issue=2 |pages=863–873 |doi=10.1007/s00414-024-03386-x |doi-access=free |pmid=39627577|pmc=11850424 }}</ref> However, Japanese craniums can be distinguished from Filipino craniums, with the former having higher upper facial height and shorter inferior malar lengths on average.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hayashi |first1=Atsuko |last2=Pietrusewsky |first2=Michael |title=Discriminant function analysis of craniometric data for distinguishing Japanese and Filipino crania |journal=Australian Journal of Forensic Sciences |date=3 September 2023 |volume=55 |issue=5 |pages=621–644 |doi=10.1080/00450618.2022.2057589 |hdl=10125/105029 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Nasal height and breadth were also greater and smaller than those found in Filipino craniums respectively although Thai craniums exhibited greater nasal height and breadth than those found in Japanese craniums. Japanese craniums also differ from Southeast Asian craniums by exhibiting adaptation to dry and cold climates and orbits that are more circular and rhomboid in shape.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":7">{{Cite journal |last1=Kongkasuriyachai |first1=Natthamon Pureepatpong |last2=Prasitwattanaseree |first2=Sukon |last3=Case |first3=D. Troy |last4=Mahakkanukrauh |first4=Pasuk |date=2020 |title=Craniometric estimation of ancestry in Thai and Japanese individuals |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00450618.2020.1789219 |journal=Australian Journal of Forensic Sciences |volume=54 |issue=3 |pages=294–310 |doi=10.1080/00450618.2020.1789219 |via=Taylor & Francis Online|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Japanese craniums also greatly differ from populations like 'typical' [[Western Australia#Demographics|Western Australians]], with the latter having longer and taller skulls and narrower frontal bones. On average, they also have greater maximum cranial length and basion-nasion length and lower mastoid height. However, Japanese exhibit similarities with North American populations in terms of their mandibular shape, which is attributed to their malleability after puberty or inter-individual variability within the Japanese population. Overall, population-specific facial morphology is established prenatally, beginning at the second trimester, before gradually diverging via growth-related processes.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Torimitsu |first1=Suguru |last2=Nakazawa |first2=Akari |last3=Flavel |first3=Ambika |last4=Swift |first4=Lauren |display-authors=3 |date=2024 |title=Estimation of ancestry from cranial measurements based on MDCT data acquired in a Japanese and Western Australian population |journal=International Journal of Legal Medicine |volume=138 |issue=3 |pages=1193–1203 |doi=10.1007/s00414-024-03159-6 |pmid=38252284 |pmc=11003893 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Katsube |first1=Motoki |last2=Vora |first2=Siddarth R. |last3=Bortolussi |first3=Stephanie |last4=Yamaguchi |first4=Yutaka |display-authors=3 |date=2025 |title=Comparative study of fetal facial shape between Japanese and North American populations using micro-CT |journal=Scientific Reports |volume=15 |issue=42009 |page=42009 |doi=10.1038/s41598-025-26185-9 |pmid=41290923 |pmc=12647795 |bibcode=2025NatSR..1542009K }}</ref>
* According to Nakao et al. (2024), there is more overlap between Kofun and Yayoi craniums than with Jōmon craniums, with Kofun craniums exhibiting high similarities with contemporary migrants from the Korean peninsula. These craniums are typically characterized by shorter anterior–posterior lengths.<ref name="PP">{{Cite journal |last1=Nakao |first1=Hisashi |last2=Kaneda |first2=Akihiro |last3=Tamura |first3=Kohei |last4=Noshita |first4=Koji |last5=Nakagawa |first5=Tomomi |date=2 April 2024 |title=Macro-Scale Population Patterns in the Kofun Period of the Japanese Archipelago: Quantitative Analysis of a Larger Sample of Three-Dimensional Data from Ancient Human Crania |journal=Humans |language=en |volume=4 |issue=2 |pages=131–147 |doi=10.3390/humans4020008 |doi-access=free |issn=2673-9461}}</ref>
